data.title: FCUS: Market Timing ETF With High Volatility
data.description:
FCUS strategy

Pinnacle Focused Opportunities ETF (NYSEARCA:FCUS) is an actively managed multi-asset fund launched on 12/29/2022. FCUS has 31 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.69%, a 12-month distribution yield of 13.64% and an expense ratio of 0.81%.

As described by Pinnacle ETFs, 30 stocks are selected by a proprietary model in the largest 1,000 U.S. equity securities. The model is based on three factors in equal weight: momentum, relative strength and earnings revisions.

Additionally to the stock model, the fund uses two market risk algorithms. If both market signals are positive, 100% of asset value is allocated in the 30 stocks. If only one signal is positive, the stock allocation is reduced to 75%, and 25% of asset value is invested in treasuries, cash equivalents or a bond index fund. If both signals are negative, the portfolio is invested 50% in stocks and 50% in bonds or cash equivalents.

The portfolio is reconstituted at least on a monthly basis, and when the market risk algorithms point to a change in asset class allocation. The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was very high: 441%.

This article will use as a benchmark the Russell 1000 Index, represented by iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Portfolio

As of writing, the portfolio has 76% of asset value in stocks and 24% in a Treasury bond fund, meaning one of the two market risk signals is bearish.

The heaviest sector in the portfolio after Treasuries is Information Technology (15.1%), although with a much lower weight than in the Russell 1000 (29.3%). A note of caution: the portfolio has only 30 stocks and is reconstituted monthly. Therefore, the sector breakdown may change quickly and should not be considered as typical of the strategy.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are moderate. The top 10 stock holdings, listed below, represent 33.1% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 3.5%.

Fundamentals

FCUS has little to show on the fundamental side. It is more expensive than IWB based on valuation ratios, and its aggregate growth rates are inferior to the benchmark, as reported in the table below.

Data: Fidelity

Performance

The track record of FCUS is too short to assess the stock model and the market timing strategy. Nonetheless, we can call it a bad start. FCUS has underperformed IWB by 7.6 percentage points in annualized return since 1/5/2023, with a much deeper maximum drawdown and much higher volatility (measured as the standard deviation of monthly return in the next table).

FCUS vs. competitors

The next table compares characteristics of FCUS and five of the largest ETFs aiming to mitigate risks by mixing equities and treasuries:

  • Pacer Trendpilot US Large Cap ETF (PTLC)
  • iShares Core Growth Allocation ETF (AOR)
  • Pacer Trendpilot 100 ETF (PTNQ)
  • WisdomTree U.S. Efficient Core Fund ETF (NTSX)
  • HCM Defender 500 Index ETF (LGH)

Some of them use timing rules, others have a fixed asset class allocation.

FCUS has few assets under management, weak liquidity and the second-highest expense ratio. It is second to last in total return since January 2023, but as I wrote before, this time frame is too short to compare returns. Nonetheless, it includes a market downturn and it is relevant to compare risk metrics. In this regard, FCUS is the riskiest of these funds, with the highest volatility by far, and a maximum drawdown more than twice deeper than any other one.

Takeaway

Pinnacle Focused Opportunities ETF (FCUS) has a portfolio of 30 stocks and an optional bond allocation based on a market-timing model. The track record of FCUS is too short to assess its performance, but it is long enough to look at risk metrics. FCUS shows a very high risk compared to a stock benchmark and competitors, based on maximum drawdown and price volatility. Additionally, its total assets and liquidity are weak, and fees are expensive. Therefore, I give FCUS a sell rating (nonetheless, I don't recommend short-selling).

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Consumers Continue To Power Front-Running Tariff-Palooza
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high-frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market". In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

April data included the ISM non-manufacturing index, which rebounded and remained positive, as did the economically weighted combined manufacturing and non-manufacturing index.

In the rearview mirror, Q1 unit labor costs

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.26%, up +0.06% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.38)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.39%, up +0.08% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.93%, up +0.02% w/w (1.36-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10-year minus 2-year: +0.39%, down -0.09% w/w (-1.07 – +0.53)
  • 10-year minus 3-month: +0.08%, up +0.09% w/w (-1.65 – 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.44%, up +0.06% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.89%, down -0.01% w/w (6.11-7.52).

Treasury and corporate bond yields are well below their 12-month highs of several months ago, as are mortgage rates. Nevertheless, they remain negative. The spreads between Treasuries and investment-grade and high-yield bonds, which blew out three weeks ago, have returned to their previous range.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +13% to 151 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4-wk avg. down -2 to 144 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +13% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4-wk avg. +9% [NSA]
  • Refi apps up +11% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +51% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB).

  • Up +0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.2% YoY.

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now higher YoY, but are close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month-high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. March data was just released two weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.2%, YoY Real M1 up +0.8% (3-year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +1.7% (3-year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when the real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it is either during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (Q1 90% actual + 10% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet) at p. 30

  • Q1 up +0.72 to 63.89, down -2.9% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The “neutral” band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. That happened one week ago for Q1, which means this metric turned negative. But as earnings have come in, it has improved sharply from its original -8% range. It could turn back to neutral by the time Q1 earnings are all reported. ***NEUTRAL***

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index unchanged (loose) at -0.44 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) up +0.01 (less loose) to -0.51(-0.50 - -0.69)
  • Leverage subindex down -0.03 (looser) to -0.21 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive is poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. Recently, this Index went below -0.60, warranting a rating change to positive, but they have since retreated back into the neutral range. Despite its recent big upward move, the leverage subindex is still positive for the economy.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down -15 to 387, up +37 m/m (209 5/24/24 – 402 5/2/25) Components: Yield curve 39 Phillips curve 348
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.0712 to -0.3478 (+0.0944 4/18/25 - -.9762 1/31/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +10.3 to +49.0 as of 5/1/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is a recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal), which explains why it remains negative. Effective next week, my colleague who compiles this score is disaggregating the two components.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive—until a month ago, when it recorded its worst reading since the 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank. It has now mainly recovered.

Since last April, IM has stated that its system does not forecast a recession.

Trade weighted US$

  • Down -0.39 to 122.86 w/w, up +0.3% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 – 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Up +0.80 to 100.37 w/w, down -4.7% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01 – 109.66) (new 12-month low intraweek).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the last couple of months. The broad US$ retreated under that threshold four weeks ago, and remains neutral, while the trade weighted US$ has been declining sharply, and in the past four weeks was even more sharply lower YoY, so a positive—so long as it remains orderly.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +1.31 to 102.76 (93.30 – 107.71)
  • Up less than +0.1% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 141.65, up +.0.56 w/w (130.47 -173.08)
  • Down -10.5% YoY.

Both of these measures improved earlier last year, and industrial commodities surged to new 12-month highs in the autumn before backing off. The broad measure then turned back negative, and has since oscillated between that and neutral. Two months ago, the industrial measure weakened to neutral for one week before rebounding, and then fell all the way to negative. Four weeks ago, the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but rebounded into a neutral range three weeks ago. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remains very negative.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Down -0.8% to 5659.91.

We had an all-time high eleven weeks ago. If there is no new high in the next 10 days, this indicator will turn negative due to the big low in April.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week) (no reports this week)

  • Empire State up +6.1 to -8.8
  • Philly down -42.3 to -34.2
  • Richmond down -9 to -13
  • Kansas City up +1 to -11
  • *Dallas down -19.9 to -20.0
  • Month-over-month rolling average: down -5 to -18.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. The indexes varied sharply last year, but recently became more variable, first turning neutral and then one month+ ago to positive. This may have reflected manufacturers’ attempting to front-run new tariffs—which would account for the sharp reversals in March and so far, this month as well.

Four weeks ago, I wrote that:

“I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.”

Those reports for April are posted below:

  • Empire State down -0.5 to -19.8
  • Philly up +1.3 to +6.9
  • Richmond down -16 to -30
  • Kansas City up +3 to +3
  • Dallas down -8.1 to -19.4
  • Average: -12.

Contrary to the above non-manufacturing reports, the ISM non-manufacturing report turned more positive in April.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 228,000, down -13,000 w/w, down -0.4% YoY
  • 4-week average 227,000, up +1,000 w/w, up +5.8% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis, FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. Should the four-week average climb above 10% higher YoY, that would warrant a change of rating to negative, but there is no indication as of now of any imminent changes.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Up 1 to 84 w/w
  • Down -6.5% YoY (low -13.7%- high -6.5%.)

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, but not enough to change its negative rating yet—until last week, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, so it is now neutral.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $267.9 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $279.7 B last year, down -$7.8 B or -4.2%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and the trend continued this year with the exception of three weeks—until April 15, which may have been affected by a change in the way Treasury recorded electronic payments that day. Until this goes out of the mix next week, consider the data to have an asterisk. Nevertheless, it scores negatively this week.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil up +$2.49 to $60.89 w/w, down -17.2% YoY ($58.21 – $83.88) (new 4-year low)
  • Gas prices up +$0.01 to $3.15 w/w, down -$0.50 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average up +3.5% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined to the bottom 1/3rd of their 3-year range, and so turned positive several months ago. With the crash in commodity prices several weeks ago, they were now very positive. The reason I score oil prices this way, as opposed to other commodities, is their huge effect on the consumer wallet.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but in March, it retreated to neutral for several weeks before turning negative four weeks ago. Last week it turned back positive.

Note: Given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3-year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.29% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), down -0.10% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record of how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including them in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.23 to 2.50 w/w (Low 1.48 May 4, 2024 – high 3.21 November 23, 2024)

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and – surprisingly – came very close to setting a new 12-month-high four weeks ago before backing off.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry | OpenTable

  • 7-day average up +6% YoY, vs. +8% one week ago.

Since 2023, this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general, this year the YoY trends have improved, but with a great deal of noise.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +6.9% YoY, 4-week average +6.2% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; low 4.8% February 1, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It has had very good readings for the past few weeks. Part of this was probably the yearly change in the week of the Easter holiday, but its continued strong performance is probably consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation.)

  • Up +0.25% to +1.62% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 – Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say, it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons have been turning higher since September, but have been very low in the last several months.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +5.3% YoY (high – 6.0% 4/25/25; 12-month low -0.1% 2/17/24) (new 12-month high).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads to employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12-month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12-month high before backing off.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +5.6% YoY
  • Intermodal units up +9.4% YoY
  • Total loads up +7.6% YoY.

(Graph at Railfax Report – North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex down -17 to 2055
  • Baltic Dry Index down -82 to 1316.

Rail data has been very volatile since early 2023, with lots of volatility from positive to negative and back again. For many weeks, it was neutral, and it has continued to be noisy week to week, and mixed for many weeks before turning positive two months ago. The big increases in the past month may reflect businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Per port sources, docking at West Coast ports has already declined, so this may well turn sharply in the next several weeks — but not this week!

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising almost relentlessly since, setting repeated 12-month highs in the past three months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded in the past nine weeks back into its middle range, and remained in that range this week.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +1.1% w/w
  • Up +1.4% YoY.

This metric oscillated between positive and negative for most of 2024. In the past several months, it was negative before rebounding to positive for three weeks. It then faded to neutral and back to negative for the past several months, but in the past several weeks, it has improved to neutral and now positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10-year Treasury ✓
10-yr – 2-yr Treasury ✓
10-yr. – 3-mo. Treasury ✓ X
2-yr Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓ X
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 5 4 5
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities ✓
Industrial commodities ✓
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 5 6 3
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓ X
Tax Withholding ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 7 2 1

.

The long leading indicators remain neutral. Q1 corporate profits, which started out estimated to be very negative, have now improved to neutral.

The short leading indicators had been negative for three weeks before returning to positive one week ago. They remained so this week, helped very much by gas and oil prices.

The coincident indicators remained positive, as consumer spending continues to hold up nicely. So far, rail traffic shows no evidence that the trans-Pacific decline in shipping due to the trade war is having an effect.

Aside from new orders, there is no sign producers are pulling in their horns. And consumers continue to enjoy a spending tailwind so long as inflation remains tame.

data.title: Spectrum Brands Holdings, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Spectrum Brands Holdings, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: American Tower's Quiet Phase May Be Ending - Why It Still Has Legs To Run
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

American Tower Stock’s (AMT) relative YTD gains over the broader market have been mostly encouraging. However, the past few months have been mostly sluggish, with the cell-tower REIT trading in a narrow, range-bound band. On the operational front, though, there’s a lot to be pleased by with AMT, with its Q1 report underscoring its resilience. Despite the Spirit churn, its Indian exit, and forex headwinds, AMT’s top-and-bottom-line results have been mostly encouraging, backed by a robust outlook. Additionally, the company is riding three powerful secular tailwinds in soaring mobile data demand, 5G infrastructure expansion, and edge computing acceleration. In terms of pricing, we see that based on forward estimates, AMT stock’s apparently lofty valuation appears a lot more digestible. Plus, my dividend discount model [DDM], using reasonable assumptions, lines up with Wall Street and suggests solid double-digit upside.

A Quick Look at the World’s Largest Tower REIT

American Tower Corporation is the undisputed king in the wireless communications infrastructure space, with a footprint covering five continents in North America, Latin America, Europe, Africa, and Asia. It boasts a market cap north of $100 billion, efficiently leasing height and space on 150,000 cell towers to the biggest mobile carriers, network operators, and broadcasters. Though its core property business continues to drive the lion’s share of sales, it has been steadily growing its services arm, which reinforces its robust digital positioning. Naturally, a lot of AMT’s success is rooted in long-term secular trends that will continue pushing its top-and-bottom-line forward.

A Look At AMT’s Potent Dividend Profile

American Tower’s dividend profile is mostly impressive, with an attractive growth profile, a modest yield, along with some leverage-related considerations.

AMT is currently yielding 3.1%, which is a lot less generous than the sector median at over 5%. However, it more than makes up for that with its superb consistency, led by a 12-year streak in annual increases, along with a 5-year dividend CAGR of 11%. Also, its 10-year dividend CAGR of close to 16% dwarfs the 2.12% sector norm, earning it an impressive A-Grade from Seeking Alpha in terms of growth and consistency.

In terms of coverage, its cash dividend payout ratio of roughly 57.4% compares favorably to the sector median of 65%. However, the situation changes when looking at it from a GAAP perspective (a 169% payout ratio), signaling reliance on non-cash adjustments and potential one-offs. Nevertheless, its AFFO payout ratio at 62.5% TTM highlights ample cushioning against its 4.8% AFFO yield (beating the 7.25% sector median). Also, interest expense is mostly contained, with AMT’s 3.42 times interest coverage, coming in ahead of the 1.90 times sector benchmark. Hence, income-focused investors may find AMT’s yield modest, but strong dividend growth and solid interest coverage help effectively balance the case.

Q1 Earnings Discussion

AMT’s Q1 results were mostly solid, with total sales of $2.56 billion, beating consensus estimates by a healthy $20 million. Property revenues were up 3% YOY, despite the 300 basis points drag from forex-related headwinds. The scenario was different on the bottom line, though, with AMT posting an EPS of $1.04, missing estimates by a sizable 56 cents. However, its adjusted EBITDA rose an encouraging 1.9% to $1.74 billion, while its AFFO per share of $2.75 rose roughly 7.1% on an adjusted basis, ahead of Mr. Market’s forecast by a comfortable margin.

Earnings took a hit on the back of multiple headwinds, which are likely to ease out in the upcoming quarters. For starters, there’s the Sprint churn, which is still weighing in on organic billings. Moreover, there was the India divestiture, where AMT sold off its Indian portfolio last year, cutting a significant share of its recurring sales. Hence, there’s the near-term FFO drag on AMT’s financials, which should ease out when the capital is deployed elsewhere. In fact, leaving the Indian market could potentially be a boon for AMT, effectively reducing forex risk, while focusing on high-return mature markets.

I guess what stood out in AMT’s Q1 results was its underlying leasing momentum. Organic tenant billings rose 4.7%, mostly in line with the management’s 5% annual target. Growth in core markets like the U.S. and China came in at 3.6% (5% excluding the Sprint churn), and a superb 6.7% internationally. These results are a modest acceleration from the REIT’s late-2024 showing, highlighting strong recurring demand for tower co-location and amendments. Moreover, on the services side, AMT continues to fire with revenue doubling YOY, and applications increasing 30% sequentially.

On the datacenter front, CoreSite added 11 megawatts of new capacity, indicating powerful leasing and pricing dynamics, while delivering consistent mid-teen yields once fully ramped. The company management believes the segment is part of its high-return growth engine along with its tower portfolio. It’s also important to note that the new capacity went live in late Q1 and should take a quarter or two to fully lift FFO.

Moreover, AMT bumped its full-year guidance recently, following its strong Q1 results. Its AFFO per share is now expected to fall in the $10.35–$10.54 range (midpoint $10.45 vs. $10.50 consensus), up 0.4% from prior guidance, which is effectively mirrored by a 0.4% lift in adjusted EBITDA, now seen at $6.885–$6.955 billion (midpoint $6.92 billion vs. $6.90 billion estimate).

Is AMT Fairly Priced? A Closer Look at Valuation

Following its slide from September highs at around $243.56, AMT stock has spent much of late 2024 and early 2025 in a corrective trough around $170 to $180. However, since early January, the stock’s mounted a superb recovery, rallying near the $220 to $225 region, as investors gain confidence in its 5G-driven leasing momentum. The last few months, though, have been relatively choppy, as the stock’s lagged the broader market.

At this time, the stock’s trading at a healthy premium to its real estate competition across most multiples. For instance, its non-GAAP TTM PE ratio is at 33.9x, roughly 21% higher than the 28.1x sector median. Moreover, its price-to-sales ratio of 10.1x is handily above the 4.1x median.

Looking ahead, though, AMT’s consensus FFO estimates show that the stock is a lot more attractive than the current metrics suggest. Analysts expect its FFO per share to drop to $9.47 this year, roughly a 15% drop YOY compared to 2024, before rebounding to the mid-teens by 2030. At the same time, its forward FFO multiple is expected to compress from roughly 23x this year to roughly 15x by 2029.

It is important to note that the credibility of these estimates wanes the further out you look. For instance, you have seven analysts covering 2025 estimates, which drops to a lone forecaster by 2029. Nevertheless, the estimates suggest that a lot of AMT’s plans are likely to come to fruition, bolstering its FFO in the process.

In digging even deeper, I built a three-stage DDM based on Seeking Alpha’s forward dividend estimates through 2027. Moreover, the inflation-adjusted S&P 500 return of roughly 7% was taken along with a 4.4% CAGR (FY1-FY3) to estimate the intrinsic value. From Year 3 and beyond, a 4% perpetual growth rate is estimated, which ultimately results in an intrinsic value of $245.63. Notably, Wall Street’s price targets sit at around $245.75, which is in line with the DDM estimate, while representing a 13% upside from current levels. Hence, given the results, AMT stock appears a lot more attractive, offering considerable upside.

Risks To The Thesis

Despite the relatively impressive trajectory, there’s a lot to unpack in AMT’s bull-case. For starters, as discussed earlier, the stock trades at a steep premium, roughly 20 times its cash flow versus the sector’s mid-teens. Though forward estimates assuage those concerns, it's still a challenge for investors to process, especially in the current scenario.

On the operational end, it’s important to note that the surge in organic billings mostly hinges on carriers’ 5G capex, and potential pullbacks in spending could seriously impact results. Also, its much-talked-about CoreSite business is up against some serious competition from wholesale co-location players and hyperscalers. Additionally, the company’s balance sheet leverage remains a major concern (net debt/EBITDA ~5×; debt/equity 447%), hampering its financial flexibility.

Takeaway on American Tower Stock

Overall, there’s a lot to like about AMT stock, as it looks to capitalize on an array of long-term wireless trends in 5G densification, mobile data expansion, and edge computing. Its operations are a peach, which deliver 5% to 6% organic cash flow expansion, backed by multi-year leases and a fast-growing services segment. Its dividend profile remains in excellent shape, marked by consistency, growth, and sustainability. Also, as mentioned earlier, its valuation looks much more compelling from a multi-year perspective, and my DDM-based fair value suggests solid upside from current levels.

data.title: 2 Of My Favorite Dividend Stocks Paying 7% And 8%
data.description:
Introduction

I have followed the market every single day since I was 15. Next month, that's almost 15 years ago.

One of the reasons why I was so fascinated by trading and investing (I started as an active trader) is the show Million Dollar Traders, which launched in 2009 on the British BBC channel.

It was about a hedge fund millionaire (Lex van Dam) letting a group of total amateurs with nothing but a short education and a good mentor (Anton Kreil) trade the market.

The fascinating thing about it was how different personalities approached the market. This included an elderly gentleman who had spent decades as an engineer, who struggled tremendously because, unlike mathematics and engineering, markets don't always seem to make sense.

This is one of the big reasons why I became a long-term investor. After having made every single mistake, you can think of at a young age, trading leveraged products and other toxic crap, I stopped caring about the little details and spent time figuring out the 'big picture' instead.

After all, the longer we stay invested, the higher the odds we end up making money, as the data in the overview below shows.

I try to improve my risk/reward by investing in stocks with the most tailwinds, as obvious as that may sound. As I don't buy index funds, I need to justify the time and effort I spend on research by beating the market.

For now, this has worked out well, as I have spent years working on my 'higher for longer' thesis and the rotation from growth to value stocks that I expect to emanate from it.

In this article, I'll update you on my thesis, explain why the market is trading so unpredictably (it's not an exact science, as we just discussed), and I will give you two of my favorite high-yielding stocks that I consider to be perfect for this environment.

So, let's keep this intro somewhat short and get right to it!

Higher-For-Longer

I brought the Million Dollar Traders example up because of just how tricky the market is for anyone trying to predict short-term market movements.

Even the Fed admitted it on Wednesday, as the world's most powerful central bank has no idea where we are headed in terms of inflation and growth. Just like the average Jane and Joe on the market, uncertainty is simply too high for Powell and his team to make clear predictions.

With unemployment still low and demand steady, Fed officials have said they are comfortable keeping rates unchanged until they have a better understanding of where the economy is headed. Powell repeated that sentiment Wednesday, adding the cost to waiting is fairly low. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

I believe John Authers summed it up nicely when he brought up just how impactful tariffs can be on both prices (inflation) and growth.

The Fed’s precise words were that “the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen” since it last meeting. That was before the Liberation Day tariff announcement on April 2 and all the excitement that followed. Few can sensibly disagree with this, or with Chair Jerome Powell’s protestations that everyone would have to “wait and see.” - Bloomberg

Although I don't care about short-term market movements, especially not from a few days ago, I believe Wednesday's price action showed perfectly just how forward-looking markets are.

While the 'higher for longer' comments from the Fed were bearish, the news of looser chip export restrictions caused the market to bet on lower tariff risks, causing the market (Nasdaq) to rise above pre-FOMC levels.

The market is playing a somewhat risky game, as it still expects three rate cuts this year. This is despite the fact that cutting rates for the Fed is much harder than for foreign central banks, as the impact of tariffs on their prices is purely deflationary. It pressures their economic growth without the inflationary component.

In the United States, both growth and inflationary risks are elevated, although the market believes the main risk is growth.

As we can see below, the implied probability of a Fed funds rate of less than 3.75% by the end of this year (the current range is 4.25% to 4.50%) is nearly 80%.

A big reason for this optimism is the deflationary pressure from slowing economic growth and factors like the oil price crash. The problem is that both of these factors are likely to be short-lived, which continues to make inflation a wild card.

While current economic turmoil and the negative effect it has on consumer spending and business investments are deflationary, I continue to believe that none of the structural factors have changed, causing me to believe we are still in for a 'higher for longer' interest rate and inflation environment.

That's why I continue to focus on companies that allow investors to do well, even if inflation and uncertainty are here to stay.

Also, none of these stocks are very direct bets on higher inflation, as all I want is to improve my risk/reward without turning my portfolio into a vehicle to bet on very specific economic outcomes.

Even if I'm completely wrong and inflation comes down and stays down, I'm unlikely to run into trouble with my investments.

Just like Formula 1, it's all about fine-tuning a car (portfolio) without making radical changes whenever one feels like change is coming.

MPLX LP (MPLX) – The Gift That Keeps On Giving

I bet most people who saw the title of this article knew I was going to bring up a midstream company, as this continues to be my favorite industry to buy high-quality income.

  • Midstream companies own the pipelines and related support (I.e., gathering and processing) infrastructure of oil and gas producers. This means their income is mainly based on fixed fees, which tremendously lowers commodity price risks.
  • Because of long-term growth in both fossil fuel supply and demand, midstream companies have favorable long-term tailwinds.
  • After many years of aggressive investments, midstream companies are focusing on free cash flow generation, which reduces the number of new projects and improves the pricing power of existing assets.

Especially in light of current economic uncertainty, I believe midstream stocks are perfect. This includes MPLX, a company I would have bought many years ago if MLPs (Master Limited Partnership) that issue K-1 forms weren't such a big issue for non-American investors.

While many of my readers love K-1s, I simply cannot invest in them, which breaks my heart every time I research MPLX, as this company is perfect for my portfolio.

MPLX is the former spin-off from Marathon Petroleum (MPC), which explains why it owns so many assets in the Midwest. MPC owns more than 60% of MPLX. This relationship is mutually beneficial, as MPC receives substantial distributions (dividends) and MPLX has a strong partner with aligned interests.

On top of that, it has two massive benefits that make it such a standout:

  • It is consistently investing in new pipelines, most of which are located in high-demand areas with very promising long-term demand growth.
  • The company has the perfect mix of growth and income, as it yields 7.8% with a five-year CAGR of 6.1%. Its most recent distribution hike on October 29 was even higher at 12.5%. Since 2021, the average annual distribution growth rate has been 10.7%. Despite this, it has a very healthy distribution coverage of 1.5x, as rapid distribution growth is backed by strong earnings growth.

It also maintains a healthy balance sheet with $2.5 billion in cash and a 3.3x leverage ratio, well below its 4.0x target, which has allowed the company to spend capital on buybacks as well.

Moreover, to maintain elevated growth, the company is engaging in actions like bolt-on acquisitions. This includes buying the remaining stake of the BANGL natural gas liquids pipeline in the Permian Basin, which is expected to close in July and make MPLX an even bigger player in America's largest oil and natural gas liquids basin.

On top of the BANGL deal, it doubled its stake in the Matterhorn Express Pipeline, which improves the much-needed takeaway capacity of natural gas in the Permian.

And in March of this year, MPLX expanded its crude oil gathering capabilities through the acquisition of assets from Whiptail Midstream in the San Juan Basin (Four Corners region).

According to the company, these assets deepen its strategic relationship with Marathon Petroleum by providing additional crude supply optionality.

The company now handles more than 10% of all natural gas produced in the United States, which is great news, as I'm very bullish on natural gas demand.

In 2025, the company is looking to spend $1.7 billion on CapEx, with 85% of this going to natural gas and natural gas liquids services. These investments are expected to generate mid-teens annual returns, supporting the company's mid-single-digit EBITDA growth targets.

When adding its 8% yield and the fact that it trades at just 8.6x operating cash flow, which is below the 9-10x multiple I apply to high-quality midstream companies, I see a path to 12-14% annual returns as a base case.

That's why I would own MPLX if I could, as I think it's very hard to find a better high-yield player with a better growth/value mix.

Altria Group (MO) – The Better Consumer Staple

I own PepsiCo (PEP), which is one of my favorite consumer staples.

However, like many of its peers, it has entered an unfavorable environment of subdued consumer sentiment, persistent input cost inflation, and supply chain headwinds (it's a global snack player).

While I absolutely love PepsiCo (I will likely cover it soon), the new stars of the consumer staples sector are producers of tobacco and alternatives.

Altria, which has the Marlboro rights in the United States, has made a huge comeback after its stock price had been under pressure for years.

The company, which yields 6.7%, has a five-year dividend CAGR and a track record of nearly 60 consecutive annual dividend hikes, as it's also one of the most reliable and consistent dividend payers on the market.

This outperformance is remarkable, as 'big tobacco' is defying the headwinds caused by long-term weakness in traditional tobacco sales and the never-ending stream of new rules around the world to combat these unhealthy products.

Just by looking at the overview below, we see that its traditional brand, Marlboro, has become one of many products that Altria sells, with an increasing focus on non-traditional and smokeless products.

As of 1Q25, the company continues to stand strong, as it even grew its market share in the premium cigarette segment (Marlboro) by 0.1 points to 59.3%.

However, the standout performer was on, which grew shipment volumes by 18% to more than 39 million cans, as we can see below. This increased its retail share to 8.8%, which is up 1.8 percentage points on a year-over-year basis.

According to the company, within the nicotine pouch subcategory, on! Now has a 17.9% market share, an improvement of 0.5 percentage points.

Unfortunately, the road to success in its e-vapor business, which it serves through brands like NJOY, remains more bumpy, as it is dominated by illegal operators who dump cheap products on the market. These illegal products have a 60% market share.

To combat these issues, the company is working with regulators and innovating to make its product stand out as much as possible. After all, that's all one can do, as this is an issue that needs to be addressed both by legal operators and the government.

This includes combating illegal Chinese e-vapor, faster authorization of legal e-vapor, and similar measures to shift the market share in favor of companies like Altria.

With all of this in mind, analysts expect 3-5% annual EPS growth, which is enough to support inflation protection for its dividend and continue its long-term growth streak.

With regard to its valuation, when adding its dividend, we get an annual return outlook of 10-12% without valuation tailwinds. If we include that MO shares trade at 11.7x earnings, below the long-term average of 13.9x, we get an even higher total return potential.

All things considered, I like MO for income and safety, especially in the current economic environment of uncertainty.

If I had an income-focused portfolio, I would definitely own MO stock.

Takeaway

Fifteen years of watching the markets has taught me (at least) one thing, which is that predicting short-term moves is a fool's errand.

That's why I made a complete U-turn many years ago, focusing on long-term investing with the incorporation of 'the big picture' instead of getting lost in the noise.

Given new macroeconomic developments and Federal Reserve comments, I believe my "higher for longer" thesis remains intact.

And in this uncertain environment, I'm leaning into high-yield stocks with strong tailwinds, which I expect to be perfect for the years ahead.

MPLX is a standout, as it's a midstream powerhouse with an 8% yield, healthy growth, and a fortress balance sheet.

If it weren't for the K-1 headache, it would be a core holding of mine.

Meanwhile, Altria is defying the odds in the traditional tobacco industry, as it is leveraging its dominance in smokeless products to deliver steady income growth and potential for capital gains.

Both are built to weather volatility, provide elevated income, and offer solid returns, even if my macro view turns out to be wrong.

Risks To My Thesis

Given the anti-cyclical revenue streams of these two stocks, the biggest risk is a general recession/stagflation scenario. While it will likely cause MPLX and MO to outperform the market, it could cause short-term stock price declines.

Moreover, both are prone to regulatory risks, as the cigarette and pipeline industries are highly regulated.

data.title: Monster Beverage: The Big Boy Seems To Be Struggling
data.description:
I've talked about Monster Beverage (MNST) a couple of times already—in fact, here's the last article I wrote this January. In it, I expressed quite a bit of skepticism about the company and mentioned that, given the underwhelming performance of its products, capital allocation would have to make the difference when it comes to total shareholder returns.

Since then, the stock has performed much better than the S&P 500—up 16% compared to a 5% drop in the index over the same period.

Since it’s an article analyzing results, it will be shorter than others where I explain the full thesis.

Thesis Recap

Growth seems to be returning to the category—in fact, other players like PepsiCo (PEP) and Celsius (CELH) have mentioned that the energy drinks sector is the only one currently experiencing positive growth, after a tough Q3 and Q4 in 2024. This point forms one of the main pillars of the Monster thesis.

As I mentioned in my previous article:

"As you well know, Monster is a company that has never had a negative sales year. It can be considered non-cyclical from the demand side but cyclical from the cost side: aluminum prices, fuel, transportation freight... These generally end up impacting its gross margin and profit, as they fall under the cost of goods sold. This is why a particular phenomenon occurs, which I will explain at the end when discussing this company's valuation."

In the end, Monster managed to achieve positive sales growth of +4.9%, something that seemed very unlikely back in the summer, as last year both the American consumer and convenience stores (the company’s largest distribution channel, accounting for over 65% of sales) had a very tough year.

Capital Allocation

Let’s move on to analyze Monster’s capital allocation—the very topic that gave my last article its title. In 2024, Monster didn’t make any acquisitions, as usual it didn’t pay out dividends, and lastly, it carried out the largest share buyback in its history.

If you remember, I was very critical of this point:

"In June of this year, Monster conducted a $3 billion tender offer to repurchase shares of the company at $53 per share. At first glance, this might not sound bad, but two key points must be considered. First, the company's share price, more than six months later, is $52. Second, and much more concerning, they executed this tender offer right before releasing results for a very poor quarter, which caused their shares to drop 12% that week, even hitting a low of -17% ($43).

If you ask me, the timing was terrible—not because of the purchase price itself, but because management knew they were about to present a very poor quarter and still decided to buy back shares at a much higher price. This could have created significantly more value for shareholders. While this difference may not be noticeable in the long term, it doesn’t reflect well on the company’s decision-making regarding capital allocation. In fact, let’s explore some alternative solutions."

Almost a year later, the stock price is up 13%, which is a good result for a company of this type over a one-year period. Still, my negative opinion regarding the timing hasn’t changed, and significantly more value could have been created if the buybacks had been done at a lower price.

And if we update it to the latest quarter, we have the following:

Share buybacks: No share repurchases were made in the first quarter; $500 million remain pending authorization. Literally the same amount as over the past five years.

Debt reduction: Although the balance sheet shows $200 million, it was fully repaid in April.

Cash position: Increased to $1.9 billion compared to $1.5 billion in December 2024.

However, we do have some positive news: ROIC grew for the second consecutive year and now stands at 32%. The best part is that this wasn’t due to an increase in NOPAT (which could still come in the future), but rather to an improvement in the denominator (Sales/Invested Capital), which I find harder to achieve.

The cash position at the end of the fiscal year was $1.5B, and for the first time in Monster’s recent history, the company issued debt—specifically to carry out the share buybacks. In any case, the net debt/EBITDA ratio is negative (it holds nearly five times more cash than debt).

Earnings Review

First of all, they present the highlights right at the top. And the sales!? Of course, since they dropped 2.3%, they don’t even mention them…

Second, they mentioned a ton of adjustments throughout their entire presentation. They've been negatively affected by forex (40% of their sales come from outside the U.S.) and by the flop of the alcoholic beverages segment they acquired in 2022. I'd say another impairment is coming, as just in this half-year alone, sales in that segment have declined by 38%. I didn't like that acquisition at all when they made it back in 2022.

Up to this point, all these might be understandable—the real issue is when they give you a laundry list of a thousand things, including that “the weather was bad” in January, to explain the weakness in sales.

Margins have indeed increased:

Gross Margin: from 54.1% to 56.5%. We've said it a thousand times—Monster isn’t cyclical in terms of sales (it has never had a year of negative sales), but it is cyclical in terms of costs. Now that raw material prices are low (look at the chart—back in 2022 it was the opposite), margins are rising. On top of that, they implemented price increases, which have a direct positive impact on margins.

Operating Margin: From 28.5% to 30.7%. Good cost control across most expense categories, except for general and administrative expenses, which increased as a percentage of sales—from 11.4% to 12.3%. After a rather poor half-year, management is making sure to line their pockets.

They’ve raised prices, but volume is starting to take a hit—though last year’s comparable base (+16%) was extremely tough. In fact, at the retail level, according to them, the category grew +10% YoY this quarter, compared to +8.7% YoY for the Monster brand and +6.9% YoY for their entire portfolio. As I have mentioned in my Celsius article, retail sales are much more important than the sales reported by the company.

Although they made a comment about energy drinks in general that I found really interesting:

Traditionally, energy drinks have been more expensive per unit than soft drinks, which made many consumers view energy drinks as a luxury or a more expensive purchase.

However, Monster claims that this price gap has narrowed, and now soft drinks have increased prices, especially in single-serve formats. Monster has maintained a competitive pricing strategy, with selective but moderate price increases.

This brings the final consumer price of both products closer together. This is something we can observe in Pepsi’s price vs. volume algorithm.

The prices of these beverages, which offer no functional benefits (for studying, working out, or simply staying awake), have increased so much that they’re now pushing customers to opt for energy drinks instead.

There are two other parts of the report that I want to highlight.

"Net sales for the Company’s Monster Energy® Drinks segment, which primarily includes the Company’s Monster Energy® drinks, Reign Total Body Fuel® high performance energy drinks, Reign Storm® total wellness energy drinks and Bang Energy® drinks, decreased marginally to $1.72 billion for the 2025 first quarter, from $1.73 billion for the 2024 first quarter. Net changes in foreign currency exchange rates had an unfavorable impact on net sales for the Monster Energy® Drinks segment of approximately $50.8 million for the 2025 first quarter. Net sales on a foreign currency adjusted basis (non-GAAP) for the Monster Energy® Drinks segment increased 2.2 percent in the 2025 first quarter." Monster Beverage Reports 2025 First Quarter Results

"Net sales to customers outside the United States marginally decreased 1.5 percent to $733.2 million in the 2025 first quarter, from $744.1 million in the 2024 first quarter, primarily due to adverse changes in foreign currency exchange rates. Net sales to customers outside the United States, on a foreign currency adjusted basis (non-GAAP), increased 6.2 percent to $790.5 million in the 2025 first quarter. Such sales were approximately 40 percent and 39 percent of total reported net sales for the 2025 and 2024 first quarters, respectively." Monster Beverage Reports 2025 First Quarter Results.

Valuation and Conclusion

Monster is currently trading at all-time highs, at a 25x EV/EBIT multiple. It seems overly demanding to me for a player that, in my view, has been losing ground for years and has experienced a significant deterioration in its management team.

This quarterly report has been full of "orange flags," such as not including sales in the highlights for the first time—just because they declined slightly. Or listing numerous reasons, some barely relevant, to justify poor sales performance. I've lost a lot of confidence in the management team—in fact, their CEO and co-founder, Rodney Sachs, announced at the annual conference that he is retiring.

I already mentioned in the previous article that, from now on, capital allocation will be a key differentiator in improving shareholder returns. I don’t see the current management team as being prepared for this (I give several more reasons in my previous article). Combined with growth coming in below expectations and a demanding valuation, I rate the stock as a sell.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday ended marginally lower for the week, as market participants digested a slightly hawkish interest rate decision from the Federal Reserve. That was offset to an extent by positive sentiment on trade after U.S. President Donald Trump announced a deal with the United Kingdom.

The Fed's monetary policy committee on Wednesday kept the key federal funds rate unchanged, as widely expected. It also warned of a potential stagflation risk. "We think our policy rate is in a good place as we wait for further clarity on tariffs," Fed Chair Jerome Powell said in his press conference afterward.

While the Fed remains in wait-and-see mode, there was progress on trade. Trump said on Thursday that the U.S. had reached a "breakthrough" deal with the UK, which will provide increased access for billions of dollars of U.S. products. Trump also expressed optimism that China wanted to make a deal, ahead of talks between Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and representatives from the Asian nation.

The first quarter earnings season also kept chugging along this week. Walt Disney (DIS) was one of the biggest names reporting. The world's second-largest entertainment company announced plans to open its first Middle East theme park in Abu Dhabi and delivered a quarterly beat.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) slipped -0.5%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) shed -0.3%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -0.2%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Reddit (RDDT) Results - Excellent Time To Load Up The Truck.

Hubbell (HUBB): AI Tailwinds Are Driving A Demand Recovery.

CrowdStrike's (CRWD) Sky-High Price Dulls My New Optimism.

BCE Makes It Official: Why 56% Dividend Cut Is Good News.

PEO: A Closed-End Oil And Gas Fund Now Yielding 10.5%.

Palantir's (PLTR) Warning Sign You Can't Ignore Any Longer.

Limited Upside for Apollo Amid Investment Spread Pressures.

Nutrien (NTR) Earnings - Expect More Near-Term Pressure.

Marvell Technology (MRVL) Likely Lost A Major Design Win.

COPX: Possible Scrap Export Curbs Complicate Copper Trade.

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -0.2% to 41,249. S&P 500 -0.5% to 5,660. Nasdaq -0.3% to 17,929. Russell 2000 +0.1% to 2,023. CBOE Volatility Index -3.4% to 21.9.

S&P 500 Sectors

data.title: Q2 Holdings: Trim This Stock As Value Fades (Downgrade)
data.description:
While the stock market has rebounded sharply from April lows as Wall Street gears up for a flurry of new trade deals, we shouldn't get overly complacent. I'm still using valuation as the key driver of my buy and sell decisions, and I'm increasing my cash holdings as the stock market heats back up, divesting stocks that, I think, are getting too frothy.

Q2 Holdings (QTWO), in my view, is one of these names. This banking software provider is still down ~10% year to date, but its recent rebound rally (especially after a well-received Q1 earnings print) has helped the company recoup a lot of this year's losses, and the stock is still sitting on ~40% gains over the past year. The key question going forward is: can Q2 continue to justify its valuation multiples, and does it deserve being held in your portfolio?

I last wrote a buy opinion on Q2 Holdings in February, when the stock was trading in the mid-$80s. Since then, Q2 has rebounded modestly (while most SaaS peers have fallen), while the company's recent growth trends are relatively "business as usual" and hardly justify any further multiples expansion. With that in mind, I'm downgrading my viewpoint on Q2 to a neutral rating.

The core issue is that so many of Q2's peers in the software sector remain at decimated levels and offer better value, while Q2 still sits on its vulnerable premiums. At current share prices near $89, Q2 Holdings trades at a market cap of $5.56 billion. After we net off the $486.0 million of cash and $492.9 million of convertible debt on Q2's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $5.57 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Q2 has slightly raised its revenue expectations to $776-$783 million (11-12% y/y growth), barely higher than $772-$779 million previously, and also upped its adjusted EBITDA range by $5 million on both ends to $170-$175 million, implying a 22.1% margin at the midpoint.

This puts Q2's valuation multiples at:

  • 7.2x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 32x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

Here's the problem: so many of Q2's peers now sit below its valuation multiples. Companies like Salesforce (CRM), Workday (WDAY), and Adobe (ADBE) - all at a similar low/mid-teens growth pace - trade at cheaper valuation multiples.

Making the comparison even clunkier for Q2 is the fact that its gross margin profile, currently in the high 50s, sits at a deficit to these SaaS peers (in the mid/high 70s) - a reflection of the company's concentration of lower-margin, non-subscription implementation work, and also meaning the company's revenue stream is less valuable.

All in all, I see limited justification to keep holding on to Q2 Holdings in my portfolio. Take advantage of the recent upside to trim your position and reinvest the proceeds elsewhere.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Q2 Holdings' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Q2's revenue grew 15% y/y to $189.7 million, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $186.5 million (+13% y/y) while also accelerating two points versus Q4's 15% y/y growth rate. We do have to acknowledge the strong performance here in a quarter where many enterprise software companies have reported that macroeconomic conditions have slowed down the deal cycle.

Q2's ARR grew 11% y/y to $847 million, which we note exceeds its guidance for FY25: hopefully an indication that the guidance is still conservative and has room to be raised.

Another indicator of strength: Q2 Holdings' RPO, or remaining performance obligations, also grew 20% y/y to $2.3 billion, adding ~$100 million of net-new backlog within the quarter. As Q2 Holdings doesn't report billings, RPO/backlog growth is one of the most telling forward-looking indicators of the company's growth trajectory.

In particular, Q2 Holdings notes that its anti-fraud detection software tools have seen strong traction among its banking customers. Management notes that the company has a full pipeline of new customers, plus a healthy plate of renewals in Q2 that hopefully lead to more expansion deals. Per CEO Matt Flake's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Looking ahead, I do want to provide a few remarks on our outlook for the remainder of the year. I believe our business is durable due to the mission-critical nature of our products, our strong and diverse customer base, and the fundamentals of our model that have helped us execute well in other recent periods of macro uncertainty. The pandemic, rising rates, and the events in the banking sector from March of ‘23 underscored the importance of deposit gathering, and our solutions are essential in helping customers attract, retain, grow, and protect deposits. We expect that focus on deposits to persist, and it has been a tailwind to our sales efforts over the last several quarters. We have a highly strategic and diverse customer base across our solution areas, and we believe our regional and community financial institution customers play an essential role in supporting the economy, including in challenging times.

In terms of what we see here in early May 2025, we continue to see a solid pipeline for the remainder of the year, with a strong overall renewal and expansion opportunity ahead of us."

Still, we should proceed with caution here. While banks reported strong earnings in Q1 driven by healthy trading desks, most bank executives cautioned on the possibility of tariffs driving us into a recession this year (Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase (JPM), said that a recession is a direct and "likely outcome" of tariffs in April). Since the banking sector is often one of the first sectors to be hit in a recession, it stands to reason that many banks may pull back on their capex and spending plans: hurting new business momentum for Q2 Holdings. In other words, I think Q2's full-year guidance boost may be premature if we start to see banks' procurement trends changing in Q2 and beyond.

Margins remained strong. Q2's adjusted EBITDA grew 62% y/y to $40.7 million, hitting a 21.5% margin: 630bps better y/y.

As a refresher, the company's medium-term operating model calls for a minimum of 360bps of y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion through FY26, driven by economies of scale on sales and marketing spend.

Key takeaways

Though I continue to believe that Q2 Holdings offers solid, consistent near-term performance, I'm worried about the possible impact of a macro recession impacting banks' buying activity and slowing down Q2's recent growth trajectory. On top of this, the stock already sits at a questionable premium to similarly growing software peers, leaving Q2 vulnerable for a near-term correction. Start to reduce your holdings in this stock before it's too late.
